1: Now Pashhur the priest, the son of Immer, who was chief officer in the house of YHWH, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things. The institutional response to Jeremiah's Temple court sermon (Jeremiah 19:14–15) is immediate. Pashhur ben Immer is the chief officer (paqid nagid) — a Temple security official responsible for maintaining order in the sanctuary complex. His family background (son of Immer — a priestly family; cf. 1 Chronicles 24:14) and his institutional role make him a representative of the religious establishment that Jeremiah has been attacking throughout chapters 7–20. The confrontation between prophet and institutional priest is inevitable.
2: Then Pashhur beat Jeremiah the prophet and put him in the stocks that were in the upper Benjamin Gate of the house of YHWH. Physical violence against the prophet followed by the stocks (mahpekhet) — a device that locked the victim's neck, hands, and feet into a painful bent-forward position. The upper Benjamin Gate was a prominent public location in the Temple area — the stocks were placed where maximum public shame would accompany the physical punishment. The prophet who announced that Jerusalem's gates would be shut (Jeremiah 17:27) is now displayed in a gate, imprisoned and shamed.
3: The next day, when Pashhur released Jeremiah from the stocks, Jeremiah said to him, YHWH does not call your name Pashhur, but Terror on Every Side. The name-change oracle is one of Jeremiah's most dramatic pronouncements. Magor-Missaviv — Terror on Every Side — will become a refrain in Jeremiah's oracles (cf. Jeremiah 6:25; Jeremiah 20:10; Jeremiah 46:5; Jeremiah 49:29). The name Pashhur is uncertain in etymology; YHWH renames him with a title of doom. The person who attempted to silence terror of YHWH's word by physical violence will himself become an embodied reminder of terror. The announcement of this new name to Pashhur's face takes considerable courage.
4: For thus says YHWH: Behold, I will make you a terror to yourself and to all your friends. They shall fall by the sword of their enemies while you look on. And I will give all Judah into the hand of the king of Babylon. He shall carry them captive to Babylon, and shall strike them down with the sword. The specific judgment on Pashhur includes being forced to watch his friends die. The king of Babylon is named explicitly — one of the early passages in Jeremiah where Babylon is identified by name rather than as the abstract north-foe. The historical specificity signals that the judgment oracle has advanced from the general to the particular: the instrument of covenant judgment has been identified.
5: Moreover, I will give all the wealth of the city, all its gains, all its prized belongings, and all the treasures of the kings of Judah into the hand of their enemies, who shall plunder them and seize them and carry them to Babylon. The total transfer of Jerusalem's accumulated wealth to Babylon is covenant confiscation (cf. Jeremiah 17:3). The Temple treasury, royal treasury, and the private wealth of the city — all will be carried to Babylon. This was historically fulfilled in the deportations of 597 BC (2 Kings 24:13) and 587 BC (2 Kings 25:13–17).
6: And you, Pashhur, and all who dwell in your house, shall go into captivity. To Babylon you shall go, and there you shall die, and there you shall be buried, you and all your friends, to whom you have prophesied falsely. Pashhur's personal fate: exile to Babylon, death in Babylon, burial in Babylon. The false prophecy charge (nibeta lahem shaqer) completes the indictment: Pashhur was not merely a security official but a prophetic voice in his own right, and he prophesied peace. His physical death in exile will prove his prophecy false and Jeremiah's true.
7: O YHWH, you have deceived me, and I was deceived; you are stronger than I, and you have prevailed. I have become a laughingstock all the day; everyone mocks me. The sixth and greatest of Jeremiah's Confessions begins here (Jeremiah 20:7–18). The opening verb pittitani (you have persuaded/deceived me) is the same word used in Exodus 22:16 for seducing a virgin and in 1 Kings 22:20–22 for the spirit sent to deceive Ahab's prophets. Jeremiah accuses YHWH of having deceived him into the prophetic vocation — lured him in with what seemed like covenant calling, and then the ministry produced only suffering and mockery. The charge of divine deception is the most theologically daring language in the Confessions.
8: For whenever I speak, I cry out, I shout, Violence and destruction! For the word of YHWH has become for me a reproach and derision all day long. The content of Jeremiah's message — violence and destruction (chamas veshod) — is the constant refrain of his preaching. Rather than producing respect or repentance, it has made him an object of contempt. Reproach and derision (cherpah veqeles): the prophet's social status has been destroyed by his message. In an honor-shame culture, this is an existential threat — the inability to function in normal social relationships because one's reputation has been destroyed by association with an unpopular message.
9: If I say, I will not mention him, or speak any more in his name, there is in my heart as it were a burning fire shut up in my bones, and I am weary with holding it in, and I cannot. Jeremiah tries silence — the obvious solution to his social humiliation — and finds he cannot maintain it. The burning fire shut up in my bones is one of the most psychologically precise descriptions of prophetic compulsion in the Old Testament. The word cannot be contained; attempting to suppress it produces greater internal anguish than delivering it. This is the paradox of prophetic vocation: Jeremiah did not choose the calling, cannot abandon it, and suffers because of it.
10: For I hear many whispering. Terror is on every side! Denounce him! Let us denounce him! say all my close friends, watching for my fall. Perhaps he will be deceived; then we can overcome him and take our revenge on him. Terror on every side (magor missaviv) — the name Jeremiah assigned to Pashhur (v3) has become the slogan of his mockers. They throw his own prophetic language back at him as a taunt. Even his close friends (anshei shlomi, men of my peace — those he trusted as allies) are watching for his stumbling, waiting to denounce him. The intimacy of the betrayal echoes Jeremiah 11:19 (the Anathoth conspiracy) and Psalms 41:9 (my close friend has lifted his heel against me — cited in John 13:18 of Judas).
11: But YHWH is with me as a dread warrior; therefore my persecutors will stumble; they will not overcome me. They will be greatly shamed, for they will not succeed. Their eternal dishonor will never be forgotten. A sudden reversal: despite the complaints of vv7–10, confidence breaks through. YHWH is with me as a dread warrior (YHWH ki be'al gibbor ariz) — the divine warrior who fights for Jeremiah. The prophet's persecutors will stumble while pursuing him; their shame will be permanent (bushat olam). The oscillation between lament and trust is the authentic texture of Jeremiah's spiritual life — he does not resolve the tension but holds both poles.
12: O YHWH of hosts, who tests the righteous, who sees the heart and the mind, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you have I committed my cause. The covenant-appeal formula recurs from 11:20 and Jeremiah 15:15 — YHWH as the one who tests (bachen) and sees (ro'eh) the hidden interior. Let me see your vengeance: the prophet asks to witness the covenant enforcement against his persecutors. I have committed my cause to you (galiti et-rivi eleycha): the legal dispute has been formally lodged with the divine judge. Jeremiah is not taking matters into his own hands but waiting for covenant justice.
13: Sing to YHWH; praise YHWH! For he has delivered the life of the needy from the hand of evildoers. A call to praise interrupts the complaint sequence — one of the most jarring transitions in Jeremiah. The needy (evyon) — the vulnerable and powerless one whom the system oppresses — has been delivered by YHWH. This praise is eschatological in character: Jeremiah sings as if the deliverance has already happened, in the mode of prophetic certainty. The Psalms of lament regularly include this element of anticipatory praise (cf. Psalms 22:22–31 after the lament of vv1–21).
14: Cursed be the day on which I was born! The day when my mother bore me, let it not be blessed! The death-wish that follows the praise is one of the most shocking juxtapositions in prophetic literature. The oscillation from praise (v13) to curse (v14) captures the extremity of Jeremiah's psychological state. The cursing of his birthday is the most total rejection of one's existence possible in the Hebrew worldview: to curse the day of birth is to wish one had never been. Job 3 opens with the same formula; Ecclesiastes 4:3 calls it better to have never been born. These are not theological positions but expressions of unbearable suffering.
15: Cursed be the man who brought the news to my father, A son is born to you, making him very glad. The birth-announcement was a moment of great joy — a son was born (cf. Jeremiah 33:11: the voice of the bridegroom; John 16:21: a woman who has given birth no longer remembers the anguish). Jeremiah curses the messenger who brought his father the good news, because that joy inaugurated a life of unrelenting suffering. The innocent messenger becomes the target of the prophet's anguish — an expression of the wish that the joyful report had never been made.
16: Let that man be like the cities that YHWH overthrew without pity; let him hear a cry in the morning and an alarm at noon. The overthrown cities are likely Sodom and Gomorrah (cf. Genesis 19:24–25) — the paradigmatic examples of total divine judgment. To be like Sodom is to receive the worst possible fate. The cry in the morning and alarm at noon describe the sounds of attack and siege — Jeremiah wishes on the birth-messenger a life as catastrophically filled with alarm as his own has been.
17: Because he did not kill me in the womb; so my mother would have been my grave, and her womb forever great. The wish reaches its extreme: Jeremiah desires that he had died in utero. The womb as grave — the place of gestation becomes the place of burial without ever having been a place of life outside. The permanent pregnancy of the mother (verachmah harah olam) is a horrific image: the womb swollen with an unborn child who is simultaneously already dead. No birth, no life, no suffering.
18: Why did I come out from the womb to see toil and sorrow, and spend my days in shame? The Confession ends on the question that frames the entire chapter's existential crisis: why? (lamah). Not how or when but why — the teleological question about the purpose of existence. Jeremiah sees his life as characterized by toil and sorrow (amal veyagon) and public shame (boshet). He does not answer his own question; the chapter ends with the unanswered why. The Confession provides no resolution — it is a wound that the book leaves open, modeling the honest anguish of the covenant messenger in a world that rejects the covenant word.
